narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put significant effort into your application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 and LSAT score of 31. Unfortunately, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 91.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass on their first try. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your academic decile rankings from school—specifically, your position in the lowest decile for Year 3 grades stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, far more than any other element. Your Year 1 decile in the bottom rung also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about your early performance relative to peers. While your family income level played a supporting role in tipping the scales toward this outcome, your choice of full-time enrollment nudged things slightly in a positive direction, as did your undergraduate GPA, though these had minimal sway. Your LSAT score had virtually no impact either way. Compared to typical students—who average around the middle of their class in Year 1 and 3 deciles, with higher LSATs near 37 and GPAs around 3.21—your lower rankings placed you well below the norm, which amplified the model's worries. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment highlighting where your profile diverges from successful bar passers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and targeting schools with more holistic reviews. You might also seek mentorship from alumni who overcame similar early hurdles. With targeted improvements, you can reshape your path and reapply stronger—we believe in your ability to turn this around. Keep pushing forward.
